changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "From The Scrapbook V"

TextENGNew: <Why Were So Many Ritual Implements Necessary?>
changeReason: The title changes from a vague 'Alleged Importance of the Implements' to a direct question 'Why Were So Many Ritual Implements Necessary?', matching the JPN which asks 'why were so many necessary.'

TextENGNew: Almost no descriptions of the specific forms of the "ritual implements" can be found.
However, there is no question that they were real, and that they existed in a wide and diverse variety.
One source states that the implements used in the Watanagashi rite alone numbered over two hundred.
changeReason: Old says 'numbered two hundred' (exact); new says 'numbered over two hundred' (exceeding). The JPN 二百を数える means 'counted as many as two hundred,' which the new version captures more accurately with 'over.'

TextENGNew: The question this raises is: why were so many different kinds necessary?
The Watanagashi rite involved hunting a sacrifice through the "demoning away" and then feasting upon them.
changeReason: Old says 'kidnap people...dissect them, and eat them'; new says 'hunting a sacrifice...feasting upon them.' The JPN 生贄を饗す means 'feast upon the sacrifice' — the old version adds 'dissect' which isn't in this line and misses the ritual hunting framing.

TextENGNew: Dissection tools and restraining devices come to mind, but even so, two hundred seems far too many.
As a general principle, the evolution of tools is a process of striving toward an optimal form.
Once a certain level of efficiency is achieved, further evolution is typically unnecessary.
Despite this, why would so many varieties have been produced?
One cultural element that diversifies with the passage of time is entertainment.
Tools used for entertainment evolve and diversify over time.
Unlike ordinary tools, reaching the optimal form represents a dead end (stagnation), prompting the search for new goals and the branching off into numerous subtypes.
Could it be, then, that the two hundred-plus "ritual implements" also possessed an element of entertainment?
This too is merely a hypothesis, but perhaps the process of offering the sacrifice was treated as a form of entertainment—a spectacle.
changeReason: Old says 'human dissection process'; new says 'process of offering the sacrifice' with 'a spectacle' added. The JPN 生贄を捧げる過程 means 'process of offering the sacrifice' and 見世物 means 'spectacle' — the new version is more accurate to the Japanese.

TextENGNew: To keep the audience from growing bored, successive heads of the Three Families developed one novel, attention-grabbing "implement (dissection tool)" after another...@kIt is not impossible to imagine.
changeReason: Old attributes development to 'The Three Families of old'; new correctly says 'successive heads of the Three Families' (歴代の御三家当主たち). Also restructured to clarify that keeping the audience entertained was the purpose, matching the JPN 観客を飽きさせないため.

TextENGNew: All of them must be enshrined within that ritual storehouse.
Only a little longer until the night of Watanagashi.
Though its original meaning has been lost, on this night of ancient tradition, I shall uncover the secrets within the ritual storehouse.
...I cannot contain my excitement.
I cannot wait for Watanagashi.
